title: Python GUI Concepts
subtitle: Build Windows, Buttons & Beautiful Interfaces!
overview: In this session, students explore Graphical User Interface (GUI) programming using Python's Tkinter library. They learn how to create windows, add widgets like buttons, labels, and text boxes, and handle user events — turning their code into real, clickable applications!

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — screenshots of student GUIs, whiteboard diagrams, activity shots.

keyTopics: What is a GUI? — Graphical User Interface vs Command Line Interface (CLI)
Tkinter — Python's built-in GUI library, no installation needed!
Window — The main container (Tk() creates the root window)
Widgets — Building blocks: Label, Button, Entry, Text, Frame, Canvas
Layout Managers — pack() lines up widgets, grid() places in rows & columns, place() uses exact coordinates
Events & Callbacks — When button is clicked, a function is called (command= parameter)
StringVar / IntVar — Special variables that auto-update the GUI when changed
mainloop() — The heartbeat! Keeps the window alive and listening for events

resources.title: Python Turtle for Visual Coding Practice

activity.title: Build a Color Changer GUI App
parts: Tkinter (built into Python — no extra install!)
steps: Create the root window: `root = tk.Tk()` and set title
Add a Label widget with a welcome message
Write a function `change_color(color)` that sets window background
Link each button to the function using `command=`
Run mainloop() and click buttons to change the background color!
Challenge: Add an Entry box where the user types a color name and presses a button to apply it

quiz.explanation: GUI = Graphical User Interface! It's the visual part of a program — windows, buttons, icons — that users interact with instead of typing commands.

quiz.q: Which Python library is used to build GUI applications without installing anything extra?
quiz.explanation: Tkinter comes built into Python! No pip install needed. Just `import tkinter` and you're ready to build windows.

options: Runs a loop that checks for clicks
quiz.explanation: `tk.Tk()` creates the ROOT window — the main container of your app. Every widget lives inside this window.

quiz.q: Which line MUST be at the END of every Tkinter program to keep the window open?
quiz.explanation: `root.mainloop()` is the heartbeat of your app! It keeps the window open and continuously listens for user events like clicks and keypresses.

quiz.q: What is a Widget in Tkinter?
options: A building block of GUI like Button, Label, Entry
quiz.explanation: Widgets are the building blocks of a GUI! Label (shows text), Button (clickable), Entry (type text), Canvas (draw things) — all are widgets.

quiz.q: Which widget would you use to display a text message on the screen?
quiz.explanation: Label widget displays text or images on the screen. Example: `tk.Label(root, text='Hello World!')`

quiz.q: Which widget allows the USER to TYPE text into the GUI?
quiz.explanation: Entry widget creates a single-line text input box. Use `.get()` to read what the user typed!

quiz.q: How do you connect a Button to a function in Tkinter?
quiz.explanation: Use the `command=` parameter! `tk.Button(root, text='Click Me', command=my_function)` — when clicked, my_function() runs automatically.

quiz.q: What is the difference between CLI and GUI?
options: CLI uses colors, GUI uses text only
CLI is text-based commands, GUI is visual with windows and buttons
CLI is faster to build, GUI is slower
quiz.explanation: CLI (Command Line Interface) = type commands in terminal. GUI (Graphical User Interface) = click buttons, see windows. Both are ways to interact with a program!

quiz.q: Which layout manager places widgets in a TABLE format (rows and columns)?
quiz.explanation: `grid()` organizes widgets in rows and columns — perfect for forms and calculators! Use `row=` and `column=` to position each widget.

quiz.q: What does `pack()` do to a widget?
options: Removes the widget from the window
Automatically places the widget and stacks them top to bottom
Positions widget at exact pixel coordinates
Resizes the widget to fill the window
quiz.explanation: `pack()` is the simplest layout — it stacks widgets one after another (top to bottom by default). Great for simple layouts!

quiz.q: What is `StringVar()` used for in Tkinter?
options: To store a list of widgets
A special variable that automatically updates the GUI when its value changes
quiz.explanation: StringVar() is a Tkinter-aware variable! When you call `.set('new value')`, any widget linked to it (via `textvariable=`) instantly updates on screen.

quiz.q: In the Color Changer activity, what parameter in `root.config()` changes the background color?
quiz.explanation: `root.config(bg='red')` changes the window background color! `bg` is short for background. You can use color names like 'red', 'blue' or hex codes like '#FF5733'.

options: Reads and returns the current value/text from the widget
quiz.explanation: `.get()` reads the current value! For Entry: `entry.get()` returns what the user typed. Very useful for forms and input fields.

quiz.q: You want to place a button at EXACT pixel position (100, 200) in the window. Which layout manager should you use?
quiz.explanation: `place()` lets you set exact coordinates! `button.place(x=100, y=200)` puts the button exactly at pixel (100, 200). Precise but tricky for resizable windows.
